flashcards.back: Disk scheduling is the process of determining the order in which disk I/O requests are serviced to minimize seek time and improve overall system performance.
Seek time is the time taken by the disk's read/write head to move to the correct track. It is the dominant component of disk access time.
Rotational latency is the time taken for the desired sector to rotate to the read/write head position after the head reaches the correct track.
FCFS (First Come First Served) services requests in the order they arrive. It is simple but does not optimize seek time.
SSTF (Shortest Seek Time First) services the request closest to the current head position, minimizing seek time but may cause starvation.
The SCAN algorithm (also called Elevator Algorithm) moves the head from one end to the other, servicing requests along the way, then reverses direction.
C-SCAN (Circular SCAN) moves the head from one end to the other servicing requests, then returns to the beginning without servicing requests on the return trip.
LOOK is similar to SCAN but the head only moves as far as the last request in each direction, rather than going to the end of the disk.
C-LOOK is similar to C-SCAN but the head only travels to the last request rather than to the end of the disk before returning.

flashcards.front: What is starvation in disk scheduling?
flashcards.back: Starvation occurs when some requests are never serviced because other requests keep getting priority (e.g., SSTF may starve distant requests).
The elevator algorithm is another name for SCAN because it works like an elevator, servicing requests as it moves in one direction then reversing.

flashcards.front: What factors affect disk access time?
flashcards.back: Disk access time = Seek time + Rotational latency + Data transfer time. Seek time and rotational latency are the major components.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between SCAN and C-SCAN?
flashcards.back: SCAN services requests in both directions and returns on the same path. C-SCAN returns to the beginning without servicing requests on the return trip, providing more uniform wait times.

flashcards.front: Why is FCFS rarely used in practice?
flashcards.back: FCFS does not optimize seek time and can result in very long waiting times for requests, leading to poor system performance.

flashcards.front: What is 'arm sweep' in disk scheduling?
flashcards.back: Arm sweep refers to the movement of the disk's read/write head across the platter surface during SCAN and related algorithms.
